item.title: Nocturne: Blue and Silver — The Lagoon, Venice
item.lesson: Whistler came to Venice ruined. The year before, he had sued Ruskin — the same Ruskin who worshipped Turner — for writing that one of his London nocturnes was 'flinging a pot of paint in the public's face.' Whistler won the trial and was awarded one farthing; the legal costs bankrupted him. He arrived in 1879 with a commission for twelve etchings, stayed fourteen months, and while the etchings rebuilt his fortune, he painted this: the lagoon after dark, San Giorgio Maggiore reduced to a breath of silhouette, a few lights swimming in blue-green dusk. A nocturne, he insisted, borrowing the word from music, is 'an arrangement of line, form, and color first' — the place almost incidental. Almost. Night does to Venice what Turner's distance did: it dissolves everything the daylight painters itemized, and what survives the dark is the city's true minimum — water, light, and the memory of architecture. It turns out that is enough.
item.lookFor: Count the sources of light — a scatter of window-lamps, their reflections, perhaps a star. Everything else in the picture is one color breathing at different depths.
